have the same behavior? Try changing the code to this:
When you run that, you should get something like
Uncaught ReferenceError: Cannot access 'myName' before initialization.
. If you're on a newer version of Chrome, you might instead see
Uncaught ReferenceError: myName is not defined
. And on Firefox — I tested version 89.0.2 (64-bit) — you'll get
Uncaught ReferenceError: can't access lexical declaration 'myName' before initialization
So the takeaway here is that browsers update their error messages over time, which is worth keeping in mind — though honestly I find Chrome's message more intuitive:
// Uncaught ReferenceError: a is not defined
// ReferenceError: can't access lexical declaration `a' before initialization
though, the error messages don't differ much:
// Uncaught ReferenceError: Cannot access 'a' before initialization
OK, so now that we know browsers update their error messages, how do we actually tell whether

The variable is in a "temporal dead zone" from the start of the block until the initialization is processed.
If you reference the variable before it finishes initializing, it sits in the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ) — and that's exactly why you get a

So how do we actually prove that

. So we can verify it like this:
// Uncaught ReferenceError: myName is not defined
And that leads us to the conclusion: "during the middle of a variable's initialization, a TDZ — a temporal dead zone — gets created."

Everyone knows var gets hoisted — but what about let? This post digs into whether let is hoisted too, why calling it early throws a ReferenceError instead of giving you undefined, and what the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ) actually is. We'll also use a typeof trick to prove let really does get hoisted, just in a different way.
